tests.name: Writing — explain HTTP caching for non-technical readers

judge.model_response:
Imagine you have a favorite recipe for a complex pasta dish. The first time you make it, you have to drive across town to a specialty library to read the original cookbook. This takes a lot of time and effort. However, once you have the recipe, you write a copy of it on a sticky note and tape it to your fridge. The next time you want that meal, you don’t drive back to the library; you simply glance at your fridge. You get the same result much faster.

HTTP caching works exactly like that sticky

tests.raw_response:
Imagine you have a favorite recipe for a complex pasta dish. The first time you make it, you have to drive across town to a specialty library to read the original cookbook. This takes a lot of time and effort. However, once you have the recipe, you write a copy of it on a sticky note and tape it to your fridge. The next time you want that meal, you don’t drive back to the library; you simply glance at your fridge. You get the same result much faster.

HTTP caching works exactly like that sticky note. When you visit a website, your computer downloads files (like images and logos) from a distant server. Instead of asking for those same files every single time you click a new page, your browser saves a local copy.

The next time you visit that site, your computer checks its own "fridge" first. If it finds a fresh copy of the image, it loads it instantly without needing to communicate with the server across the internet. This makes websites load faster, saves data, and reduces the workload on the website's computers.

Source [C] provides the most directly applicable evidence for a startup choosing between these two policies, as it specifically compares hybrid models against fully in-office and remote arrangements. 

Source [C] recommends a hybrid model—typically two to three days per week in the office—as it maximizes both individual productivity and team cohesion. Research cited in [C] indicates that hybrid workers achieve productivity levels similar to remote workers but exhibit significantly higher scores in innovation, collaboration quality, and job satisfaction, while reducing voluntary attrition by 35% compared to in-office workers. To be effective, [C] recommends designating in-office days for collaborative tasks (brainstorming, onboarding, relationship-building) while reserving remote time for "deep individual work," and ensuring team members overlap on the same office days to avoid the pitfalls of staggered mandates.

tests.raw_response:
Evidence on remote work's productivity impact is mixed, depending on how productivity is measured. Data suggests individual output increases for knowledge workers due to fewer interruptions, reduced commute fatigue, and quieter environments [A]. Metrics such as call volume and hourly output show gains, and remote-first firms benefit from larger talent pools and lower attrition [A].

However, remote work may hinder collective productivity by fragmenting professional networks, slowing innovation, and reducing the quality of mentorship for junior employees [B]. Lack of in-person interaction can also degrade organizational culture and a sense of belonging [B].

Hybrid models appear to balance these effects, matching remote productivity levels while improving collaboration, innovation, and job satisfaction [C]. Research indicates that "deep work" is most efficient at home, whereas brainstorming and onboarding are more effective in person [C]. Maximum benefit occurs when teams overlap in-office days to facilitate coordination [C].
